items.prompt_md: In a prefix-sum array where `prefix[i]` is defined as the sum of `nums[0..i-1]`, what does `prefix[i]` represent?
options: The sum of `nums[i]` and every element after it
The sum of all elements from index 0 up to, but not including, index `i`
The single value at index `i`
The average of the first `i` elements
items.answer: The sum of all elements from index 0 up to, but not including, index `i`
items.explanation_md: `prefix[i]` rolls up everything strictly before index `i`. `prefix[0]` is the sum of an empty range (0), and each step adds exactly one more element.

items.prompt_md: After building a prefix-sum array in O(n) time, what is the time to answer a single arbitrary range-sum query `[l, r]`?
options: O(r - l)
O(log n)
O(n)
items.explanation_md: One subtraction — `prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]` — regardless of how wide the range is. That's the entire point of precomputing: pay O(n) once, then O(1) per query forever after.

items.prompt_md: When counting subarrays that sum to `k`, you keep a hash map from prefix-sum value to how many times that value has occurred so far. Why does the count matter, not just whether the value has been seen?
options: So it converts every prefix sum into a unique array index
Because two different earlier indices can share the same prefix-sum value, and each occurrence marks a valid subarray start for the current index
Because it keeps the array sorted for binary search
So negative prefix sums get rounded to zero
items.answer: Because two different earlier indices can share the same prefix-sum value, and each occurrence marks a valid subarray start for the current index
items.explanation_md: If the same prefix-sum value occurred three times before, then there are three distinct earlier starting points that each form a valid subarray ending at the current index — the count tells you how many to add, not just that at least one exists.

items.prompt_md: While scanning left to right and tracking a running prefix sum, what value do you look up in the hash map at each index to count subarrays summing to `k`?
options: `currentPrefixSum + k`
`k / currentPrefixSum`
`currentPrefixSum - k`
`currentPrefixSum * k`
items.answer: `currentPrefixSum - k`
items.explanation_md: If some earlier prefix sum equals `currentPrefixSum - k`, then the subarray between that earlier point and the current index sums to exactly `k` — that's the algebra behind the lookup.

items.prompt_md: Why is the hash map seeded with `{0: 1}` before the scan begins, rather than starting empty?
options: To handle the case where a subarray starting at index 0 itself sums to exactly `k`
To prevent the hash map from ever being empty
Because index 0 of every array is defined to be zero
To make the total subarray count always come out even
items.answer: To handle the case where a subarray starting at index 0 itself sums to exactly `k`
items.explanation_md: If `currentPrefixSum - k == 0`, that means the subarray from index 0 through the current index sums to `k`. Without the seeded `{0: 1}` entry, that valid subarray would never be counted.

items.prompt_md: An array contains negative numbers. Why does a sliding-window approach to counting subarrays summing to `k` break down here, while prefix-sum + hash map still works?
options: Sliding window relies on the running sum growing as the window expands and shrinking when you drop the left edge — negative numbers break that monotonic relationship
Sliding window can only iterate arrays of even length
Hash maps cannot store negative numbers as keys
Negative numbers cause integer overflow in every language
items.answer: Sliding window relies on the running sum growing as the window expands and shrinking when you drop the left edge — negative numbers break that monotonic relationship
items.explanation_md: Sliding window's shrink rule ('drop the left edge when the sum is too big') assumes adding elements only increases the sum. A negative element can shrink the sum on its own, so the window no longer knows which direction to move. Prefix-sum + hash map never relies on that assumption.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the prefix-sum + hash map approach for counting subarrays that sum to `k`?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: In the worst case every prefix sum is distinct, so the hash map grows to hold up to n entries.

items.prompt_md: With `prefix[i]` defined as the sum of `nums[0..i-1]`, which expression gives the sum of the inclusive range `[l, r]`?
options: `prefix[r] - prefix[l]`
`prefix[r+1] - prefix[l+1]`
`prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]`
`prefix[r] - prefix[l-1]`
items.answer: `prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]`
items.explanation_md: `prefix[r+1]` covers everything through index `r`, and `prefix[l]` covers everything before index `l`. Subtracting leaves exactly `nums[l..r]`. Getting the `+1` wrong here is the classic off-by-one bug in this pattern.

items.prompt_md: The prefix-sum + hash map approach does a hash-map lookup and insert at every single index. Why is the overall algorithm still O(n) instead of something worse?
options: Because the hash map is rebuilt from scratch after each lookup
Because hash map insert and lookup are O(1) on average, so doing n of them stays O(n) total
Because only the first and last index perform a lookup
Because the array is sorted before scanning begins
items.answer: Because hash map insert and lookup are O(1) on average, so doing n of them stays O(n) total
items.explanation_md: Each of the n indices does a constant amount of hash-map work (one lookup, one insert/update), and n times O(1) is O(n).

items.prompt_md: Suppose you accidentally look up `currentPrefixSum - k` in the hash map *before* adding `nums[i]` to the running prefix sum at each step (instead of after). What happens?
options: It has no effect on the result
It doubles every count
The lookup ends up comparing against the previous index's prefix sum, silently shifting which subarrays get counted
It only affects the first element of the array
items.answer: The lookup ends up comparing against the previous index's prefix sum, silently shifting which subarrays get counted
items.explanation_md: The order of 'update the running sum' versus 'look up and record' matters — swapping them shifts every lookup by one index, which either double-counts or drops valid subarrays without throwing any error. It's a quiet off-by-one, not a crash.

items.prompt_md: A fixed array of daily step counts doesn't change throughout the day, but a fitness app repeatedly asks for the total steps between two arbitrary hours, dozens of times.
options: Recompute the sum of the requested range from scratch on every query
Sort the array once, then binary-search for the range boundaries
Precompute a running prefix-sum array once, then answer each range query with a single subtraction
Use two pointers that walk inward from both ends of the requested range
items.answer: Precompute a running prefix-sum array once, then answer each range query with a single subtraction
items.explanation_md: Same static array, queried repeatedly for range sums — the precompute-once-answer-many-times shape. Recomputing per query redoes the O(n) scan every single time.

items.prompt_md: An array of daily stock-price changes includes negative entries (e.g. -3, 5, -1, 4). You need to count how many contiguous day-ranges have price changes summing to exactly 0.
options: Slide a window, shrinking from the left whenever the running sum exceeds the target
Track running prefix sums in a hash map and look up prefixSum − k at each index
Use two pointers moving inward from both ends of the array
Brute-force every subarray and sum it directly
items.answer: Track running prefix sums in a hash map and look up prefixSum − k at each index
items.explanation_md: Negative price changes make the window sum non-monotonic, so 'shrink when too big' no longer guarantees correctness. Prefix-sum + hash map makes no assumption about sign and stays O(n).

items.prompt_md: You must count how many contiguous subarrays of an integer array sum to exactly `k`. The array isn't guaranteed to be all-positive, and you want a single linear pass.
options: Fix the left pointer and binary-search for the matching right pointer
Recompute every subarray's sum directly
Slide a window, expanding right and shrinking left whenever the sum overshoots `k`
Track running prefix sums in a hash map and look up prefixSum − k at each index
items.answer: Track running prefix sums in a hash map and look up prefixSum − k at each index
items.explanation_md: Without an all-positive guarantee, sliding window's shrink rule isn't valid. Prefix-sum + hash map handles any mix of signs in one O(n) pass.

items.prompt_md: A dashboard array holds daily net revenue, and some days show refunds as negative values. Analysts want to know over how many day-ranges the revenue nets out to exactly zero.
options: Track running prefix sums in a hash map and look up prefixSum − k at each index
Two pointers converging from both ends of the array
Brute-force every subarray and sum it directly
Slide a window and shrink it whenever the sum exceeds `k`
items.answer: Track running prefix sums in a hash map and look up prefixSum − k at each index
items.explanation_md: Refund days introduce negative entries, so the window's sum can rise again right after shrinking. Only prefix-sum + hash map gives a reliable O(n) count here.

items.prompt_md: A fixed array of exam scores never changes, but the registrar repeatedly asks for the sum of scores between student `i` and student `j`, for many different `(i, j)` pairs.
options: Loop over the requested range and add up scores fresh for every query
Sort the scores, then binary search for the range
Use two pointers that shrink toward the middle
Precompute a running prefix-sum array once, then answer each query with one subtraction
items.answer: Precompute a running prefix-sum array once, then answer each query with one subtraction
items.explanation_md: Repeated range queries on a static array is the same trigger every time: pay O(n) once to precompute, then O(1) per later query.

items.prompt_md: For every index in an array, you need the sum of all other elements except that index, and you want to avoid rescanning the whole array for each index.
options: Precompute running totals from the left and from the right once, then combine the two per index in O(1)
For each index, loop through the whole array skipping that index
Sort the array first, then compute totals
Use two pointers converging on the index in question
items.answer: Precompute running totals from the left and from the right once, then combine the two per index in O(1)
items.explanation_md: This is the 'everything before combined with everything after' shape — one O(n) precompute on each side beats an O(n) rescan repeated for every index.

items.prompt_md: Given an integer array containing both positive and negative numbers, count the number of contiguous subarrays summing to a target `k`. O(n) extra space is fine.
options: Brute-force every subarray and sum it directly
Slide a window and shrink it whenever the running sum exceeds `k`
Two pointers moving inward from both ends
Track running prefix sums in a hash map and look up prefixSum − k at each index
items.answer: Track running prefix sums in a hash map and look up prefixSum − k at each index
items.explanation_md: Mixed-sign data rules out the window-shrink assumption. Prefix-sum + hash map stays correct and O(n) regardless of sign, and the O(n) space budget is exactly what it needs.

items.prompt_md: An hourly weather log records temperature deltas, including negative deltas for cooling hours. You need to find how many hour-ranges have a net delta of exactly `k`, scanning the log only once.
options: Track running prefix sums in a hash map and look up prefixSum − k at each index
Slide a window, shrinking from the left whenever the sum exceeds `k`
Sort the deltas, then two-pointer toward the middle
Recompute the sum of every candidate range from scratch
items.answer: Track running prefix sums in a hash map and look up prefixSum − k at each index
items.explanation_md: Cooling hours mean negative deltas, so a shrink-on-overshoot window can miss valid ranges. Prefix-sum + hash map is the safe single-pass choice.

items.prompt_md: A ledger array of daily transactions (deposits and withdrawals, so some entries are negative) is fixed for the day. An auditor repeatedly asks for the running total between any two transaction indices.
options: Two pointers walking inward from the requested indices
Recompute the total between the requested indices from scratch every time
Precompute a running prefix-sum array once, then answer each query with a single subtraction
Sort the transactions, then binary search for the boundary
items.answer: Precompute a running prefix-sum array once, then answer each query with a single subtraction
items.explanation_md: A plain prefix-sum array answers any range-sum query in O(1) regardless of sign — negatives only break the sliding-window shrink rule used for subarray-count problems, not a straight range-sum precompute.

items.prompt_md: You need to find how many contiguous subarrays sum to exactly `k` in an array that includes negative numbers, using only a single linear pass.
options: Slide a window and shrink it whenever the sum exceeds `k`
Brute-force every subarray and sum it directly
Two pointers moving inward from both ends
Track running prefix sums in a hash map and look up prefixSum − k at each index
items.answer: Track running prefix sums in a hash map and look up prefixSum − k at each index
items.explanation_md: Negative numbers plus a single-pass requirement is the canonical trigger for prefix-sum + hash map over sliding window or brute force.
